- 0.58.0-3 extract `notes/todo-backlog.md` (prioritized entries
  stay in todo.md; references re-packed per file)
- 0.58.0-4 expand CLAUDE.md `## File reads` to bugs.md / chores
- 0.58.0-5 codify in CLAUDE.md: chores title-only during cycle,
  In Progress moves into chores at close-out, problem+plan
  shape for In Progress / chores intros / Todo entries
- 0.58.0 close-out: move In Progress block into chores-12;
  `## Done` entry; update `notes/README.md`

### Outcome

Cycle grew from 3 planned commits (intro trim + Priorities +
bugs split) to 6 as the work surfaced two related concerns:
the bot's routine read cost (handled by extracting
`todo-backlog.md` and the CLAUDE.md `## File reads` rule),
and the chores/In-Progress duplication during cycles (handled
by codifying the new "chores title-only during cycle, move at
close-out" mechanic and the problem+plan shape).

`notes/todo.md` is now sized for cheap routine reads — the
head limit=60 covers intro + `## In Progress` + `## Priorities`,
the live surfaces. The split infrastructure (`notes/bugs.md`,
`notes/todo-backlog.md`, re-packed per-file refs) makes
future similar splits cheap.

## docs: extract cycle protocol (0.59.0)

Commits: [[2]]

`notes/cycle-protocol.md` becomes the canonical,
self-contained home for the cycle workflow. CLAUDE.md
keeps a 10-line pointer. Originally scoped as "verbatim
relocate"; opened up into a top-to-bottom iterative
tightening of the extracted protocol.

Late-stage design (ochid invariant + bot-repo rules +
squash gating + cross-repo migration + Ideas-aware
Preparation/Close-out) was planned for `-2` but
deferred at close-out. The rules were exercised
manually via Option F (app squash + bot-side trailer
rewrite + force-push), establishing the recipe that
`vc-x1 push --squash` (Todo #2, P1) will automate.

### As-built ladder

- 0.59.0-0 Preparation — backfill 0.58.0 chores
  `Commits:` ref; bump Cargo.toml; capture 0.58.0
  follow-up Todo (`vc-x1 push` N:1 P1); refresh
  Priorities.
- 0.59.0-0.1 capture design notes for `-1` as `###`
  subsections (see existing subsections below — they're
  the early-stage design that got deferred when 0.59.0
  pivoted to squash close-out).
- 0.59.0-1 Extract + tighten cycle protocol —
  `notes/cycle-protocol.md` (504 lines after iterative
  tightening); `notes/substep-protocol.md` recipes
  folded as `## Sub-cycle ladders`; CLAUDE.md `## Cycle
  Protocol` (~390 lines) → 10-line pointer;
  `init-clone-refactor-conflict.md` cross-ref updated;
  2 entries added to `notes/todo-backlog.md` + 42
  renumbered.
- 0.59.0 Close-out via squash + Option F —
  `## Ideas` section added to `notes/todo.md`
  (chores-convention-relocation entry #1; deferred
  codification entry #2); symmetric-squash captured as
  Todo #2 (P1); close-out bookkeeping (In Progress →
  chores; `## Done` entry); app squash; bot-side
  `jj describe` of `af60f979` (replace two `ochid:`
  trailers with one to squashed chid + rewrite note);
  force-push `.claude/main`; push app `main`.

### Outcome

cycle-protocol.md landed at significantly higher quality
than originally scoped — the "verbatim relocate" plan
opened the door to a top-to-bottom tightening pass that
improved structure throughout (phase-first overview;
`###` subsections; Chores sections + Commit description
extracted as their own `##` sections; Iterative work
pattern captured; chid/ochid defined locally).

First squash close-out via the manual Option F recipe;
first cycle to rewrite a published bot-side commit's
description. The codification follow-up that would make
the recipe binding (and surface the ochid invariant in
cycle-protocol.md) is captured as `## Ideas` entry #2.

### Squash carve-out (for CLAUDE.md `### Pushing`)

Default = merge for any multi-commit cycle. Squash = explicit
opt-in for cycles whose sub-steps are intermediate validation
points rather than distinct landings. Every cycle is ≥3
commits (Preparation + ≥1 Work + Close-out), so the
"merge of one parent" edge case is moot.

### Close-out follow-up capture (for CLAUDE.md `### Close-out`)

At close-out, write any follow-up Todos surfaced during the
cycle to `notes/todo.md > ## Todo` (if prioritized) or
`notes/todo-backlog.md` (if long-tail). List them in the
close-out commit body so the additions are reviewable.

### Close-out surfaces follow-ups (meta-note, for `## Cycle Protocol`)

Close-out often surfaces follow-up items that became visible
only by doing the cycle's work. Capture them at close-out
(per the rule above) so they survive `/exit` between cycles
— sessions don't carry conversational context across `/exit`.

## docs: consolidate notes conventions (0.60.0)

Bot is the primary writer of notes files, so the

> ## Per-commit flow > step 4` since the 0.59.0

listed seven `CLAUDE.md#…` anchors that had been removed

Cycle scope expanded to fix all three: CLAUDE.md

notes/README.md `## Workflow and conventions` breaks
cycle-protocol.md into explicit pointers (Cycles /
Per-commit flow / Commit description / Pushing);
README.md `## Contributing` is rewritten against current
anchor homes.

### As-built ladder

- 0.60.0-0 Preparation — backfill 0.59.0 chores
  `Commits:` ref; bump Cargo.toml; pick up the "notes
  conventions → CLAUDE.md" Idea into `## In Progress`;

- 0.60.0 Close-out — chores narrative; `## Done` entry +

content.text: The audit walk needs to read ~15+ source files across many subcommands. I'll delegate the breadth to a subagent and synthesize their findings into the audit doc.

input.description: por vs dual parity audit walk
input.prompt:
You are auditing the `vc-x1` Rust CLI at /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1 to find every place where the `dual` workspace topology (code repo + `.claude/` companion repo, cross-linked by `ochid:` trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) gets a privileged code path over `por` (Plain Old Repo — a single code repo, no `.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`).

This is the 0.61.0-1 audit walk of a docs-only cycle. Do NOT change any code. Write findings to `notes/por-dual-parity-audit.md` (a new file).

**Background context:**
- `por` is gated by the `--por` flag — see `src/options_flags/por.rs`.
- T5 ("por/dual parity + `dual → por` conversion") in `notes/todo.md` is the parent design item; the user said "por is bolted on; dual is primary."
- Design stub: `notes/por-dual-parity.md`.
- An earlier user proposal (start with a user-config topology default) was deferred — audit-first was the agreed approach.
- File conventions live in `CLAUDE.md` — follow the "Prose form" (intro + bullets, lines ≤72 cols) and "Speculation marker" rules (prefix mechanism/cause/prediction claims with "The bot thinks ...").

**Areas to audit (each gets its own `##` section in the audit doc):**

1. **`options_flags/por.rs`** — the `--por` gate. How is `PorFlag` threaded through commands? Which commands accept it, which don't, which need it but don't have it?
2. **`init`** — `src/init.rs` and `src/init/*`. The dual path writes `.vc-config.toml`, creates `.claude/`, may seed via `--use-template <code,bot>`, sets up two remotes. What does the `--por` path do or not do? Look for `if por`/`!por` branches.
3. **`clone`** — `src/clone.rs`. Mirror of init for an existing remote. Same questions.
4. **`push`** — `src/push.rs` and `src/push/*`. How does it coordinate app + `.claude` commits? What happens with `--por`? See `notes/todo.md` Todo #1 for known limitations (1:1 symmetric WC commits assumption).
5. **`sync`** — `src/sync.rs` and `src/sync/*`. Two-repo pull dance. `-R` flag wiring (per Done #5: "sync gains `-R`"). What's the por path?
6. **`finalize`** — find it via grep. `.claude` cadence (one push = one `.claude` commit), squash + push detach. Does por even invoke finalize?
7. **`show` / `chid` / `desc` helpers / `validate-desc` / `fix-desc`** — anything that reads or writes ochid trailers. `src/desc_helpers.rs`, `src/validate_desc.rs`, `src/fix_desc.rs`, `src/show.rs`. ochids are a dual-only construct (they refer across repos); does any of this code assume dual even when run on por?
8. **Tests and fixtures** — `src/*/tests.rs`, `src/init/tests.rs`, etc. Look for `FixturePor` vs the dual fixture (likely called `Fixture` or `CliFixture`). Coverage gaps: which subcommands have dual-only tests?

**For each area, in its section, document:**
- **Files touched** — paths (relative to repo root) and line ranges.
- **Divergence** — the specific code shape where dual is privileged. Quote brief snippets where helpful (use ``` rust fenced blocks).
- **Severity / category** — pick from:
  - *Structural*: code branches on `por`/`!por` or iterates over a dual-shaped repo list.
  - *Defaulting*: dual is implicit unless `--por` is set (vs. being a peer choice).
  - *Feature gap*: functionality exists for dual but not por (or vice versa).
  - *Coverage gap*: dual is tested, por is not (or vice versa).
- **Equalization sketch** — one-sentence note on what equalization might look like. Don't propose fixes in detail; that's for follow-up cycles. Mark with "The bot thinks ..." if it's a guess.

**Top of doc:** an intro paragraph stating purpose, scope, and that it's a snapshot at commit `463e6bd9` (0.61.0-0). Note the audit is read-only.

**Bottom of doc:** a `## Summary` section with a parity-gap table — one row per finding, columns: Area / Category / Severity (rough: low/med/high based on how much dual-shaped assumption is baked in) / Equalization size (S/M/L guess).

Keep findings concrete and file-anchored. Do NOT speculate broadly — if you can't find the code, say so rather than guess. Conform to the "Speculation marker" rule for any inferred mechanism.

Report back when the file is written. Provide a short summary (under 200 words) of the headline findings and any areas where you got stuck.

content.text:
Audit doc written at `notes/por-dual-parity-audit.md` (snapshot of `463e6bd9`, read-only). Eight sections + parity-gap summary table.

Headline findings:

- **`push` is the biggest gap** — entirely dual-baked. No `--por` flag on `PushArgs`; the state machine names `CommitClaude` / `FinalizeClaude` stages; `claude_path()` hard-codes `<root>/.claude`; commit bodies stamp `ochid: /.claude/...` / `ochid: /...` unconditionally. Running `vc-x1 push` against a por workspace would resolve nonexistent `.claude/...` paths.
- **`PorFlag` reaches only `init` and `clone`** — every other subcommand infers topology indirectly from `.vc-config.toml`. Creation-time flag, no runtime equivalent.
- **`validate-desc` / `fix-desc` are dual-required** — `other_repo_from_config` errors with "missing workspace.other-repo" on por; no topology check, no skip-when-por path.
- **`sync` is the parity model already** — `default_scope` / `scope_to_repos` (in `common.rs:569-653`) handle por cleanly: missing workspace root or empty `other-repo` → `Scope([Code])` → cwd.
- **`init` / `clone` defaulting is asymmetric** — dual is the default branch; `--por` is the opt-out; `--config` is only valid with `--por`.
- **Tests:** `FixturePor` exists but only `init/tests.rs` uses it (5x); all `push` and `sync` integration tests use the dual `Fixture` (8 + 9 uses).
- **`finalize` body is topology-neutral** — single-repo operator; only the surrounding `push` workflow makes it dual-shaped.

No areas where I got stuck; the codebase is small enough that grep + file reads covered everything cleanly.

# por/dual parity audit (0.61.0-1)

Read-only snapshot audit of every place the `dual` workspace
topology (code repo + `.claude/` companion, cross-linked by
`ochid:` trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) gets a
privileged code path over `por` (Plain Old Repo — single code
repo, no `.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`). Snapshot taken at
commit `463e6bd9` (`docs: prep por/dual parity audit
(0.61.0-0)`). Parent design item is T5 in
[`todo.md`](todo.md); design stub is
[`por-dual-parity.md`](por-dual-parity.md).

- Scope: every subcommand, the `--por` flag's reach, the
  ochid-touching helpers, test fixtures.
- Out of scope: proposing equalization shape (each finding
  carries a one-sentence sketch only — concrete fixes are
  follow-up cycles).
- Method: grep + read-through. No code changes.

## 1. `options_flags/por.rs` — the `--por` gate

content.text:
Work complete. Please review.

Summary of `-1` audit walk:

- **`notes/por-dual-parity-audit.md`** (new, 284 lines) — eight `##` sections (one per audit area) + Summary table. Snapshot pinned to commit `463e6bd9` (0.61.0-0).
- **`Cargo.toml`** — version bump to `0.61.0-1`.
- **`notes/todo.md`** — `-1` ladder step flipped to `(done)`.

**Headline findings from the audit** (full details in the doc):

- **`push` is the largest gap** (Severity High, Eq size L) — entirely dual-baked; no `--por` on `PushArgs`; `claude_path()` hard-codes `<root>/.claude`; commit bodies stamp `ochid: /.claude/...` unconditionally; running it against a por workspace would resolve nonexistent paths.
- **`PorFlag` reach is limited** to `init` + `clone` (High, L) — every other subcommand infers topology indirectly from `.vc-config.toml`. Creation-time flag, no runtime equivalent.
- **`validate-desc` / `fix-desc` are dual-required** (Medium, M) — `other_repo_from_config` errors with "missing workspace.other-repo" on por.
- **`sync` is the parity model already** (Low, S) — `default_scope` / `scope_to_repos` in `common.rs:569-653` handle por cleanly via `Scope([Code])`. Closest existing template for "topology from config, not from a flag."
- **`init` / `clone`** symmetric at the orchestrator layer but asymmetric at the defaulting layer (dual default; `--por` opt-out; `--config` is por-only).
- **`finalize` body is topology-neutral** — single-repo operator; only its surrounding `push` workflow makes it dual-shaped.
- **Tests** — `FixturePor` exists but only `init/tests.rs` uses it (5x); all `push` (8x) and `sync` (9x) integration tests use the dual fixture.

Cargo cycle: `cargo install` only (docs-only commit, no Rust changed).

Ready to commit when you've reviewed.

message.content: This'll be -1 great, but rather than closing-out next let's make -2 an analysis and see what's common and what's por|dual only and then see if we codify in -3 or close-out. Thoughts?

content.text:
Yes — `-2` analysis is a good call. The `-1` doc catalogs *divergences*; an analysis pass adds the inverse view (what's already shared) and is exactly the input you'd need before any equalization design.

**What I'd put in `-2`:**

A per-subcommand commonality view — for each of init/clone/push/sync/finalize/ochid-helpers/tests, three buckets:

- **Shared** — code that runs the same on both topologies today (e.g. `default_scope` / `scope_to_repos` in `common.rs`, the bookmark/push retry logic, `finalize`'s repo-agnostic body).
- **Dual-only** — code that assumes `.claude/` exists (e.g. `claude_path()`, ochid composition, the dual `Fixture`).
- **Por-only** — anything gated behind `--por` that dual skips (e.g. `init`'s `--config` flag, single-remote setup).

Plus one summary: which subcommands are mostly-shared with a thin dual veneer (good equalization candidates) vs. structurally dual (`push`).

**On `-3` codify:** I'd lean toward close-out, not `-3`. Codifying a design pattern (topology trait? scope-everywhere? config-driven dispatch?) in CLAUDE.md *before* prototyping it on a small target (e.g. fixing `validate-desc` to no-op on por) risks ossifying a shape that doesn't survive first contact with code. The bot thinks the right "codification" is a small follow-up equalization cycle in 0.62.0, not a CLAUDE.md rule written from analysis alone.

So my suggested updated ladder:
- `-0` Prep (done)
- `-1` Audit walk (done)
- `-2` Commonality analysis — produces `notes/por-dual-parity-analysis.md` (or appends a `## Commonality` section to the audit doc)
- Close-out — synthesize audit + analysis into ranked follow-up Todo entries; `-3` codification only if `-2` surfaces something concretely codifiable.

Which file shape do you want for `-2` — sibling analysis doc, or append to the audit doc?

message.content: append using a ## Comonality section to the audit doc

## Commonality

The audit above inventoried *divergences*. This section
inverts the view — for each subcommand, what's already
shared between por and dual, what's dual-only, what's
por-only. Equalization is cheapest where the shared bucket
is already large and the dual-only bucket is a thin veneer.

### Per-subcommand buckets

#### `chid` / `desc` / `list` / `show` — fully shared

- **Shared (all)** — these four route through the
  `CommonArgs` + `common::for_each_repo(&c.repos, ...)`
  shape (`src/chid.rs:53`, `src/desc.rs:68`,
  `src/show.rs:118`, `src/list.rs`). Repos resolve via
  `default_scope` + `scope_to_repos` (`src/common.rs:594`,
  `:618`), which already handle por gracefully
  (`Scope([Code])` when `other-repo` is missing/empty).
- **Dual-only** — none in the runtime body. The dual case
  just adds a second entry to the repo list.
- **Por-only** — none.

The bot thinks this family is the *template* for the rest
of the codebase. Scope-driven iteration is the working
model of topology-neutral dispatch.

#### `sync` — fully shared

- **Shared (all)** — same scope-driven shape as the
  `CommonArgs` family; per the 0.54.0 cleanup
  (`notes/todo.md > ## Done`) sync got `-R` and routes
  through `default_scope` / `scope_to_repos`.
- **Dual-only** — none.
- **Por-only** — none.

#### `validate-desc` / `fix-desc` — dual-only outliers

- **Shared** — title-matching, ochid extraction, and the
  per-commit walk (`src/desc_helpers.rs` exports
  `extract_bare_id`, `find_matching_commit`,
  `validate_ochid`, etc. and these are topology-neutral).
- **Dual-only** — the entry shape. Both call
  `other_repo_from_config(&config)?` directly
  (`validate_desc.rs:133`, `fix_desc.rs:152`), which errors
  on por instead of resolving to a no-op or single-repo
  validation.
- **Por-only** — none.

The bot thinks equalization here is local: convert the
dual-required prelude into a `default_scope`-style
resolution that no-ops `Side::Bot` when absent. The shared
body doesn't need changes.

#### `init` / `clone` — shared body, dual default

- **Shared** — repo-creation primitives (`init_one` /
  `clone_one` per `chores-07.md > init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)`), GitHub remote provisioning, push retries,
  template seeding mechanism, `--account` / `--repo`
  resolution via `config.rs`.
- **Dual-only** — the orchestration outer loop runs twice
  (code + bot) when `--por` is absent; `.vc-config.toml`
  write; `.claude/` directory creation; `--use-template
  <code,bot>` accepts a bot value.
- **Por-only** — `--config <none|PATH>` (overrides the
  canned `.vc-config.toml` write, only meaningful when
  there'd be one).

The asymmetry is at the *defaulting* layer (dual is the
implicit default; `--por` is the opt-out) more than the
*code* layer. The body is already roughly symmetric.

#### `push` — dual-only

- **Shared** — bookmark tracking, the push state machine's
  generic stages (`prepare`, `commit-app`, `push-app`),
  the retry/resume scaffolding around `.vc-x1/push-state.toml`.
- **Dual-only** — `claude_path()` resolution; the
  `CommitClaude` / `FinalizeClaude` stages; ochid trailer
  composition (`ochid: /.claude/<chid>` on app, multi-line
  `ochid: /<code-chid>` on bot); the `--from
  bookmark-both` flag; the 1:1 symmetric WC-commits
  assumption flagged in T1.
- **Por-only** — none.

The largest gap. The bot thinks no por code path exists
*at all* today; a por workspace running `vc-x1 push` would
resolve nonexistent `.claude/` paths during the
`CommitClaude` stage.

#### `finalize` — body shared, use site dual

- **Shared (all)** — the body is a single-repo operator
  (squash + push + cleanup). Topology-neutral.
- **Dual-only** — none in the body. The dual shape is
  *external*: `push` schedules a detached `finalize`
  against `.claude` only after `push-app` succeeds.
- **Por-only** — none.

Once `push` gains a por path, `finalize` requires no
changes.

#### Tests and fixtures

- **Shared** — `Fixture` / `CliFixture` provide a dual
  workspace; `FixturePor` provides a por workspace; both
  honor `$VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` / `$VC_X1_TEST_KEEP` and the
  RAII drop pattern.
- **Dual-only** — every integration test for `push` (8
  uses) and `sync` (9 uses); the `chid` / `desc` / `show` /
  `list` test paths (these route through `CommonArgs`,
  which works on por, but the fixtures don't cover that
  case).
- **Por-only** — five uses in `init/tests.rs` exercising
  the `--por` initialization path.

The bot thinks the test-coverage gap mirrors the runtime
gap exactly: `push` and the desc outliers have zero por
coverage because the runtime code doesn't support por
there; the `CommonArgs` family has zero por coverage
despite supporting it.

### Equalization candidates, ranked

Ordered from closest-to-shared (cheap wins) to
furthest (architectural work):

1. **`validate-desc` / `fix-desc`** — local refactor:
   replace the `other_repo_from_config` prelude with a
   scope-aware resolution that no-ops when `Side::Bot`
   is absent. Body unchanged. The bot thinks this is the
   smallest concrete equalization and a good prototype.
2. **`CommonArgs`-family por test coverage** — pure test
   work; surface bugs in scope handling without
   architectural risk. Likely lands a `FixturePor` use
   in `chid`/`desc`/`show`/`list` test modules.
3. **`init` / `clone` defaulting** — make `--por` /
   `--dual` peer flags rather than dual-default + `--por`
   opt-out; thread the chosen topology through the body
   without changing the inner primitives. User-config
   `[default].topology` (the original user proposal)
   becomes a small follow-on once peers exist.
4. **`push`** — the structural one. `claude_path()`,
   stage names, ochid composition all carry the
   dual-shape baked in. The bot thinks this is best done
   *after* `--por` becomes a runtime-known fact across
   all subcommands (i.e. after the desc outliers and
   defaulting are handled), so push has a stable contract
   to dispatch against.
5. **Topology-from-config rule** — codify (CLAUDE.md or a
   small ARCHITECTURE note) that every runtime subcommand
   resolves topology via `default_scope`, never from a
   `--por` flag. The flag stays creation-time only. The
   bot thinks this rule is the right *outcome* of the
   equalization above, not a prerequisite — codify it
   once the prototype validates the shape.

### Summary

Three structural classes today:

- **Topology-neutral via scope** —
  `chid` / `desc` / `list` / `show` / `sync` / `finalize`-body.
  This is the working pattern.
- **Topology-required (dual)** —
  `push` / `validate-desc` / `fix-desc`. These bypass
  scope and assume `.claude/` exists.
- **Topology-creating** — `init` / `clone`. The `--por`
  flag's only legitimate home; chooses the workspace
  shape that downstream commands then read from
  `.vc-config.toml`.

Equalization is "make Topology-required match
Topology-neutral via scope" — not a new pattern, just
extending an existing one. The smallest concrete win is
`validate-desc` / `fix-desc`; the largest is `push`. The
two creation-time commands stay roughly as-is, with the
defaulting layer (peer flags + optional user-config
default) as the only change.

# References

content.text:
Edits done, not committed. Ready to talk.

**`-2` adds**: `## Commonality` section in `por-dual-parity-audit.md` (+181 lines, doc now 464 total). Cargo bumped to `0.61.0-2`. Ladder in `## In Progress` updated (3→4 commits) and `-2` marked `(done)`.

The Commonality section has three layers:

1. **Per-subcommand bucket lists** — for chid/desc/list/show, sync, validate-desc/fix-desc, init/clone, push, finalize, tests/fixtures — what's shared, dual-only, por-only.
2. **Ranked equalization candidates** (5 items) — cheapest first:
   - validate-desc/fix-desc (local refactor, scope-aware prelude)
   - CommonArgs-family por test coverage
   - init/clone defaulting → peer flags
   - push (the structural one)
   - codify topology-from-config rule (after prototype validates it)
3. **Summary**: three structural classes today — *topology-neutral via scope* (the working pattern), *topology-required dual* (push + desc outliers), *topology-creating* (init/clone). Equalization = "extend the existing pattern," not invent a new one.

**Headline insight that fell out of the analysis**: the codebase already has the right pattern (`default_scope` + `scope_to_repos` + `for_each_repo`) — half the subcommands route through it cleanly. The "dual is bolted on" framing inverts when you look this way: por is bolted on **everywhere the scope pattern wasn't applied**. Push and validate-desc/fix-desc are the holdouts.

What did you want to discuss?

It also creates https://github.com/winksaville/por1

So full parity with "dual", But if you don't want .vc-config.toml or you don't want the remote repo, too bad that's not supported.
So we need to define the "features" we want and what their parity should be.

I think we should do that in -3, thoughts?

content.text:
Yes, `-3` for feature decomposition is the right call — and your example sharpens what I missed in `-2`. `--por` today is a **tangled bundle**, not an independent axis. The dimensions are conflated:

1. **Topology** — single repo vs. code+`.claude/` pair.
2. **`.vc-config.toml`** — written or not.
3. **Remote provisioning** — create GitHub repo or local-only.
4. **(possible)** — jj init, `.gitignore` template seeding, `.vc-x1/` state dir, etc.

Today: `--por` collapses #1 to single, leaves #2 still happening (just with a degenerate `[workspace] path = "/"`), and leaves #3 unchanged (still hits GitHub). The user has no way to ask for "single repo, no `.vc-config.toml`, no remote" or any of the other independent combinations.

The audit's "T5 parity" framing missed this. Parity isn't just "dual and por treated equally" — it's "the underlying axes are independently controllable, and the names dual/por are *combinations* of those axes, not primitives."

**What `-3` should produce:**

- A `## Feature axes` section in the audit doc (or a new sibling — your call) that:
  - **Names each independent axis** with current state, target state, and proposed flag.
  - **Defines the defaults** (probably: dual topology, config written, remote created — preserving today's no-arg `init` behavior).
  - **Maps `--por` and `--dual` onto axis combinations** for back-compat shorthand.
  - **Identifies what's missing** — no current flag for "no remote," no current flag for "no `.vc-config.toml`" (`--config none` is close but reads as override-not-disable).
  - **Connects to user-config** — once axes are independent, `[default].topology`, `[default].create-remote`, `[default].write-vc-config` are obvious follow-on knobs (and the original user proposal lands cleanly here).

**One caution:** keep `-3` to *defining* the axes, not implementing them. The implementation lands in 0.62.0+ equalization cycles. Otherwise `-3` swells past a docs commit.

**Ladder update:**

- `-0` Prep (done)
- `-1` Audit walk (done)
- `-2` Commonality analysis (done)
- `-3` Feature-axes decomposition — name the independent axes, defaults, mapping from `--por`/`--dual`, gap list
- Close-out — synthesize all three layers (where divergences live × what's already shared × what axes should exist) into ranked follow-up Todo entries

The bot thinks this three-layer view (divergence × commonality × feature-axes) is the right input for 0.62.0 to make concrete equalization choices, where 0.61.0 alone wouldn't have given.

Want me to go ahead with `-3` on those lines?

input.old_string:
defaulting layer (peer flags + optional user-config
default) as the only change.

# References
input.new_string:
defaulting layer (peer flags + optional user-config
default) as the only change.

## Feature axes

The audit and commonality passes treat `--por` and `--dual`
as the primitives. They aren't — they're bundles. The
concrete shape `vc-x1 init --por <name>` produces today is
"single repo, with a (degenerate) `.vc-config.toml`, pushed
to a freshly-created GitHub repo." Each of those is an
independent choice, but the user can only ask for them as a
bundle.

This section names the independent axes, says what each
controls, lists today's flags and defaults, and identifies
gaps. **No implementation — defining only.** Equalization
across these axes lands in 0.62.0+ cycles.

### Axes

#### A1. Topology

- **States** — `single` (one code repo) | `dual` (code repo
  + `.claude/` companion cross-linked by `ochid:`).
- **Today** — dual is the implicit default; `--por`
  is the boolean opt-out. No `--dual` peer.
- **Surface** — `--por` (boolean, `init` and `clone` only).
  Downstream commands infer from `.vc-config.toml >
  [workspace] other-repo` via `default_scope`.
- **Gap** — `--dual` doesn't exist as a peer flag, so the
  defaulting is asymmetric: you can be explicit about por
  but not about dual.

#### A2. `.vc-config.toml` write

- **States** — `written` (canned content) | `not-written`
  | `overridden` (caller-supplied path copied in).
- **Today** — written by default. `--config none|<path>`
  selects `not-written` or `overridden`, **but is rejected
  unless `--por` is also set** (`init.rs:610`: "--config
  is only valid with --por (dual configs are per-side and
  unconditional)").
- **Surface** — `--config <none|PATH>`.
- **Gap** — not orthogonal to A1. The dual path forces
  `written` with canned content; no way to override.
- **Constraint** — dual `other-repo` resolution reads
  `.vc-config.toml`; `(A1=dual, A2=not-written)` would
  break runtime topology detection. So the matrix may
  legitimately forbid that combination, but the error
  shape should be "you asked for an impossible
  combination," not "this flag is only valid with `--por`."

#### A3. Remote provisioning

- **States** — `github-create` (default; creates
  `winksaville/<name>` via gh CLI) | `github-skip` (URL
  is non-GitHub or pre-existing) | `local-bare` (`git
  init --bare` under `--repo local=<dir>`) | `none`
  (no remote configured at all — just the working repo).
- **Today** — `--repo <cat>[=<val>]` resolves to one of
  the first three via the user-config `[account.<a>.
  repo.category]` lookup; `none` doesn't exist.
- **Surface** — `--repo` for the cat/val, `--private` for
  GitHub visibility, `--push-retries` /
  `--push-retry-delay` for the post-create push.
- **Gap** — no flag for "no remote." A user wanting a
  local workspace (or to provision the remote separately)
  has no clean way to express it.

#### A4. Private vs public

- **States** — `public` (default) | `private`.
- **Today** — `--private` flag.
- **Surface** — `--private`.
- **Scope** — only meaningful when A3 = `github-create`.
- **Gap** — none structurally; the flag is independent
  and orthogonal.

#### A5. Template seeding

- **States** — `none` (default) | `code-only <path>` |
  `code-and-bot <path,path>`.
- **Today** — `--use-template <CODE[,BOT]>`. Second value
  is dual-only.
- **Surface** — `--use-template`.
- **Gap** — none structurally; per-side templating is
  naturally A1-aware. Bot-side template under A1=single
  is meaningless and rejected today.

#### A6. Working-tree scaffolding (jj init, `.gitignore`)

- **States** — `on` (default) | `off`.
- **Today** — always `on`. No flag.
- **Gap** — probably not worth a knob. jj is the workspace
  scaffolding; opting out doesn't yield a meaningful
  workspace. `.gitignore` is unconditional and a fixed
  content list. The bot thinks A6 stays a non-axis
  unless a concrete use case surfaces.

### Defaults summary

| Axis | Default | Today's flag | Independent today? |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| A1 Topology | dual | `--por` (opt-out only) | No (no `--dual` peer) |
| A2 `.vc-config.toml` | written | `--config <none\|PATH>` | No (rejected when dual) |
| A3 Remote | github-create | `--repo <cat>[=<val>]` | Mostly (`none` missing) |
| A4 Private | public | `--private` | Yes |
| A5 Template | none | `--use-template <C[,B]>` | Yes (A1-aware) |
| A6 Scaffolding | on | (none) | Not an axis today |

### Mapping `--por` / `--dual` onto axis combinations

Today `--por` and (implicit) `--dual` are *bundle
shorthands*. After axes are independent, they remain as
shorthands for the common combinations:

- `--dual` (or no flag — back-compat) → `(A1=dual,
  A2=written, A3=github-create, A4=public, A5=none)`.
- `--por` → `(A1=single, A2=written, A3=github-create,
  A4=public, A5=none)`. Note A2=written is what today's
  `--por` actually does, not `not-written` (the
  `.vc-config.toml` is still written, just degenerate).

A user wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't
spell it; tomorrow it would be `--por --config none --no-remote` (or
shorthand `--bare` if that combination is common enough to
deserve one).

### Connections to user-config

Once axes are independent, each maps to a `[default].*`
key in `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`, so the user-config
proposal that opened this cycle lands cleanly:

- `[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`
- `[default].write-vc-config = true | false | "<path>"`
- `[default].remote = "github" | "local" | "none"`
- `[default].private = true | false`

`--account`-scoped overrides ride on the existing
`[account.<a>]` substrate. The bot thinks A3 is the most
useful default to make user-configurable (different
accounts → different remote providers), with A1 second.

### Gap list (input for close-out)

The concrete gaps for follow-up cycles to seed Todos
against:

1. **A1 has no `--dual` peer** — asymmetric defaulting.
   Add `--dual` (alias of "no `--por`") for explicit
   parity; allow `--por`/`--dual` exactly one of.
2. **A2 errors on dual** — change the error to
   "(A1=dual, A2=not-written) is impossible because dual
   needs `.vc-config.toml` for runtime topology" rather
   than the current flag-restriction message; allow
   `--config <path>` (override) under `--dual`.
3. **A3 missing `none`** — add a way to spell "no remote."
   Probably `--repo none` (orthogonal to the existing
   `remote`/`local` categories) or a separate `--no-remote`
   flag. The bot thinks `--repo none` keeps the
   one-knob-for-A3 shape.
4. **A6 is non-negotiable today** — confirm at close-out
   whether to leave it that way; if so, drop A6 from the
   axis list.
5. **User-config keys** — once A1–A5 are independent,
   wire `[default].topology` / `.write-vc-config` /
   `.remote` / `.private` into the `init` resolution
   chain. Follow the three-step `resolve_repo` shape
   (CLI → `[default]` → error).

These five become candidate `## Todo` entries at
close-out, ranked by the equalization-candidate ordering
from `## Commonality` (axis fixes that overlap with
`validate-desc` / `fix-desc` equalization land cheapest).

## Feature axes

The audit and commonality passes treat `--por` and `--dual`
as the primitives. They aren't — they're bundles. The
concrete shape `vc-x1 init --por <name>` produces today is
"single repo, with a (degenerate) `.vc-config.toml`, pushed
to a freshly-created GitHub repo." Each of those is an
independent choice, but the user can only ask for them as a
bundle.

This section names the independent axes, says what each
controls, lists today's flags and defaults, and identifies
gaps. **No implementation — defining only.** Equalization
across these axes lands in 0.62.0+ cycles.

### Axes

#### A1. Topology

- **States** — `single` (one code repo) | `dual` (code repo
  + `.claude/` companion cross-linked by `ochid:`).
- **Today** — dual is the implicit default; `--por`
  is the boolean opt-out. No `--dual` peer.
- **Surface** — `--por` (boolean, `init` and `clone` only).
  Downstream commands infer from `.vc-config.toml >
  [workspace] other-repo` via `default_scope`.
- **Gap** — `--dual` doesn't exist as a peer flag, so the
  defaulting is asymmetric: you can be explicit about por
  but not about dual.

#### A2. `.vc-config.toml` write

- **States** — `written` (canned content) | `not-written`
  | `overridden` (caller-supplied path copied in).
- **Today** — written by default. `--config none|<path>`
  selects `not-written` or `overridden`, **but is rejected
  unless `--por` is also set** (`init.rs:610`: "--config
  is only valid with --por (dual configs are per-side and
  unconditional)").
- **Surface** — `--config <none|PATH>`.
- **Gap** — not orthogonal to A1. The dual path forces
  `written` with canned content; no way to override.
- **Constraint** — dual `other-repo` resolution reads
  `.vc-config.toml`; `(A1=dual, A2=not-written)` would
  break runtime topology detection. So the matrix may
  legitimately forbid that combination, but the error
  shape should be "you asked for an impossible
  combination," not "this flag is only valid with `--por`."

#### A3. Remote provisioning

- **States** — `github-create` (default; creates
  `winksaville/<name>` via gh CLI) | `github-skip` (URL
  is non-GitHub or pre-existing) | `local-bare` (`git
  init --bare` under `--repo local=<dir>`) | `none`
  (no remote configured at all — just the working repo).
- **Today** — `--repo <cat>[=<val>]` resolves to one of
  the first three via the user-config `[account.<a>.
  repo.category]` lookup; `none` doesn't exist.
- **Surface** — `--repo` for the cat/val, `--private` for
  GitHub visibility, `--push-retries` /
  `--push-retry-delay` for the post-create push.
- **Gap** — no flag for "no remote." A user wanting a
  local workspace (or to provision the remote separately)
  has no clean way to express it.

#### A4. Private vs public

- **States** — `public` (default) | `private`.
- **Today** — `--private` flag.
- **Surface** — `--private`.
- **Scope** — only meaningful when A3 = `github-create`.
- **Gap** — none structurally; the flag is independent
  and orthogonal.

#### A5. Template seeding

- **States** — `none` (default) | `code-only <path>` |
  `code-and-bot <path,path>`.
- **Today** — `--use-template <CODE[,BOT]>`. Second value
  is dual-only.
- **Surface** — `--use-template`.
- **Gap** — none structurally; per-side templating is
  naturally A1-aware. Bot-side template under A1=single
  is meaningless and rejected today.

#### A6. Working-tree scaffolding (jj init, `.gitignore`)

- **States** — `on` (default) | `off`.
- **Today** — always `on`. No flag.
- **Gap** — probably not worth a knob. jj is the workspace
  scaffolding; opting out doesn't yield a meaningful
  workspace. `.gitignore` is unconditional and a fixed
  content list. The bot thinks A6 stays a non-axis
  unless a concrete use case surfaces.

### Defaults summary

| Axis | Default | Today's flag | Independent today? |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| A1 Topology | dual | `--por` (opt-out only) | No (no `--dual` peer) |
| A2 `.vc-config.toml` | written | `--config <none\|PATH>` | No (rejected when dual) |
| A3 Remote | github-create | `--repo <cat>[=<val>]` | Mostly (`none` missing) |
| A4 Private | public | `--private` | Yes |
| A5 Template | none | `--use-template <C[,B]>` | Yes (A1-aware) |
| A6 Scaffolding | on | (none) | Not an axis today |

### Mapping `--por` / `--dual` onto axis combinations

Today `--por` and (implicit) `--dual` are *bundle
shorthands*. After axes are independent, they remain as
shorthands for the common combinations:

- `--dual` (or no flag — back-compat) → `(A1=dual,
  A2=written, A3=github-create, A4=public, A5=none)`.
- `--por` → `(A1=single, A2=written, A3=github-create,
  A4=public, A5=none)`. Note A2=written is what today's
  `--por` actually does, not `not-written` (the
  `.vc-config.toml` is still written, just degenerate).

A user wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't
spell it; tomorrow it would be `--por --config none --no-remote` (or
shorthand `--bare` if that combination is common enough to
deserve one).

### Connections to user-config

Once axes are independent, each maps to a `[default].*`
key in `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`, so the user-config
proposal that opened this cycle lands cleanly:

- `[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`
- `[default].write-vc-config = true | false | "<path>"`
- `[default].remote = "github" | "local" | "none"`
- `[default].private = true | false`

`--account`-scoped overrides ride on the existing

content.text:
Strong agreement on the principle, and an observation: **this is already the working pattern in the codebase**. `src/config.rs`'s `resolve_repo` does exactly this for `--account` and `--repo`: CLI → `[default].account` → `[account.<a>.repo].default` → error with the exact key to set. Extending it to A1 (and A3–A5) isn't a new rule, it's *consistency* — the existing precedent just hasn't been applied to topology yet.

**Resolution chain for A1, mirroring `resolve_repo`:**

1. `--por` or `--dual` on the CLI → use that.
2. `[default].topology` in `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` → use that.
3. Otherwise error: `"no topology specified; set [default].topology = \"single\"|\"dual\" or pass --por / --dual"`.

Exactly-one-of `--por`/`--dual` (clap-enforced). Internal value lives as an enum, not a boolean — kills the `--por`/`!--por` framing that the audit found everywhere.

**Why the principle is right across all axes** (not just A1):

- For A1 / A3: the consequences of "wrong default" are real (got a `.claude/` you didn't want; pushed to a GitHub account you didn't mean to). Explicit forces the choice once.
- For A4 (private/public): today's silent default-public is a **security smell**. Making it explicit is a feature, not friction.
- For A5 (template): default = none is so universal it barely counts as a "default" — but the principle still applies cheaply.
- For A2 (`.vc-config.toml` write): once dual *requires* it and single makes it *optional*, the axis collapses to one knob (`--config none|<path>|written`) that's only meaningful under single. Probably resolve A2 *after* A1, with "not specified → written" being the only safe single-side default.

**Two implications worth flagging now:**

- **Test fixtures rewrite.** Every integration test in `init/tests.rs`, `push/tests.rs`, `sync/integration_tests.rs` runs without explicit topology today. Once it's required, all tests must spell it. Tractable — likely a global `--dual` add or a fixture-level default — but real work for the equalization cycle.
- **Bootstrap UX.** New user runs `vc-x1 init foo` cold, gets an error. Error message must be the doc — pointing at the exact key + showing the canned shape. The existing `section_error` helper in `config.rs:345` is the model; reuse it.

**One small disagreement / nuance:**

For A6 (jj init / `.gitignore`) I'd still drop it from the axis list entirely — not "default = on, must be set in config," but "this isn't a meaningful knob at all." `vc-x1 init` without jj is `git init`; `vc-x1 init` without the canned `.gitignore` is the user typing four lines manually. Neither variation is useful enough to maintain. The bot thinks A6 stays non-axis; calling it out as such in the doc is fine.

**Open question I'd push back on:** for A2 specifically, should `[default].write-vc-config` even exist? The constraint matrix says dual *forces* written; single *allows* not-written. So `[default].write-vc-config = false` is silently overridden under dual. That's a footgun — the default says one thing, the chosen topology overrides it. Either:

- (a) Make it strictly under-single (A2 doesn't appear when A1=dual); user-config knob only kicks in when single is chosen.
- (b) Drop the user-config knob entirely; A2 is a per-invocation choice via `--config` only.

The bot leans (a) — keep the knob, but document that it only applies under single. (b) loses the "set it once" benefit you're after.

Want to lock in A1 as I sketched and move to A2?

message.content:
I tend to agree with you, but other nuances.
1) I specifically want to be able to convert an existing por to a dual
2) When you have a dual and add --por treat it as a por and ignore vc-config.toml (local and global)? Answer: Y
3) Should a por ever use vc-config.toml or create vc-config.toml (local and global)? Answer: N if the above is Y
Thoughts?

content.text:
Good probing questions. Let me untangle one ambiguity and then react to each.

**Ambiguity first**: "ignore vc-config.toml (local and global)" — there are two distinct files with similar names:

- **Workspace** `.vc-config.toml` at the repo root — written by `init`, defines `[workspace] path = "/"` and `[workspace] other-repo`. Workspace metadata.
- **User-config** at `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` — defines `[default].*`, `[account.<n>].*`. User defaults.

The audit / commonality work has only really touched the *workspace* file. Your "local" must be workspace; "global" is presumably user-config. I'll separate them in my reactions.

---

**(1) `por → dual` conversion** — agreed, that's T4 today. Once por is fully `.vc-config.toml`-free (per #3 below), conversion is clean: it just writes `.vc-config.toml` + creates `.claude/` from scratch. No "upgrade a degenerate config" needed.

**(2) Dual workspace + `--por` flag → treat as por, ignore workspace `.vc-config.toml`** — agreed, with one nuance. This means `--por` becomes a **runtime override**, not just creation-time. Today it appears only on `init`/`clone`; under your rule it'd appear on every subcommand and short-circuit `default_scope` → `Scope([Code])` regardless of what the workspace file says. That's actually useful — escape hatch for "this command, just my code, leave `.claude/` alone." Worth doing.

**(3) Should por ever use or create `.vc-config.toml`?** — agreed: **No** for the *workspace* file. If `--por` runtime-overrides everything, the workspace file's only purpose is dual's `other-repo` lookup, and por never needs that. Today's `vc-x1 init --por foo` writing a degenerate `[workspace] path = "/"` file is residue from when `--por` was a half-implemented opt-out. Drop the write entirely.

---

**One distinction I'd push back on, though**: I'd keep por reading the **user-config** (`~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`). Otherwise por loses:

- A3 — `[default].account = "home"` so `vc-x1 init --por foo` knows where to push without retyping `--repo remote=...`.
- A4 — `[default].private = true` so a "private-by-default" user gets it on por too.
- A1 — the very rule we just locked in: "if topology is not specified, look in `[default].topology` before erroring." A naked `vc-x1 init foo` user-config-says-"single" should produce por, no flag needed.

The bot thinks the right framing is:

- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml`** — dual-only. Por never reads, never creates. (Your #3 = Y under this reading.)
- **User-config `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`** — topology-neutral. Both por and dual consult it for defaults across all axes.

The two files have different jobs: workspace is *runtime topology detection*, user-config is *user defaults*. Dropping the first for por is correct; dropping the second would gut the "set it once" benefit of user-config that motivated `-3` in the first place.

**Does that distinction match what you meant?** If you meant "por ignores both files" I'd want to hear the case for cutting user-config too — I might be missing something.

Based on your suggestion por & dual "always" use global I think we need a --no-global-config cli to force them to ignore them.
Also, we should support VC_X1_<ENV> for every global and local config?

content.text:
Both useful — let me sketch the layered shape they push us toward, then flag scope and one piece I'd cut.

---

**Resolution chain becomes 4 layers (with an explicit-required floor):**

1. **CLI** — `--por`, `--account`, `--config <path>`, `--global-config <path>`, etc.
2. **Env-var** — `VC_X1_<KEY>` overrides config files.
3. **Local config** — `./.vc-config.toml` or `--config <path>`.
4. **Global config** — `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` or `--global-config <path>` (or pointed at by local).
5. **Error** — "no `<key>` specified; here are the 4 surfaces" (the floor we agreed on in A1).

Same shape as today's `resolve_repo`, just with env-var inserted between CLI and config-files, and local added between env-var and global. Each layer is *optional*; you can use any subset.

---

**Reactions to your two asks:**

**(a) `--no-global-config`** — agreed. Necessary for tests / CI / "ignore my defaults this once." For symmetry I'd also add **`--no-local-config`** — but flag that today it would only do what runtime `--por` already does (ignore workspace topology), because local config carries nothing else. Add it as a peer once local config gains non-topology fields; until then it's redundant with `--por`.

**(b) `VC_X1_<KEY>` env-var support** — agreed, with a flat naming convention to mirror the resolved-key namespace:

- `VC_X1_TOPOLOGY` → A1 (`single`/`dual`)
- `VC_X1_ACCOUNT` → A3 account
- `VC_X1_REPO` → A3 category[=value]
- `VC_X1_PRIVATE` → A4 (true/false)
- `VC_X1_USE_TEMPLATE` → A5
- `VC_X1_CONFIG` → path or `none` (overrides local)
- `VC_X1_GLOBAL_CONFIG` → path (overrides global)
- `VC_X1_NO_LOCAL_CONFIG`, `VC_X1_NO_GLOBAL_CONFIG` → boolean

Precedent: `config.rs` already honors `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`; tests already use `VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` / `VC_X1_TEST_KEEP`. So `VC_X1_*` is established.

---

**Two concerns worth raising before we accept:**

**Scope creep.** `-3` was scoped to "name independent feature axes." We're now scoping a 4-layer config-resolution system, which is **its own design exercise**. The bot thinks two reasonable moves:

- (i) Document this in the existing `## Feature axes` section as the **resolution chain** subsection — concise (the per-axis sections already say "CLI → user-config → error"; this just slots env-var + local + `--no-global-config` into the picture). No code in `-3`; defining only.
- (ii) Spin a sibling note (`notes/config-layering.md`) to keep the audit doc focused, and reference it.

I lean (i) — it's the same shape, just one more layer; the audit doc is the natural home. If it grows past ~50 lines we split.

**One piece I'd cut: "global config path defined in local `.vc-config.toml`."** You mentioned "defined in local .vc-config.toml" as one of the global-config sources. I'd drop that:

- It's a third path to specify the global, on top of `--global-config` and `~/.config/`. Three ways to do one thing.
- It enables "team-wide shared defaults" (local points at a checked-in config), but that use case is served by `--global-config $(cat .vc-config-global-path)` or a project-level shell alias.
- Removing it keeps local and global as **co-equal**, not "local can mutate the global resolution."

If you want the "team-wide shared defaults" capability, the cleaner shape is letting `--global-config` accept a relative path, so a script in the project can do `vc-x1 init --global-config ./team-config.toml ...`. No magic file-content interpretation.

---

**Want me to write up the 4-layer chain into the doc as a new `### Resolution chain` subsection under `## Feature axes`?** That captures everything we just settled and keeps `-3` from creeping further. I'd also note `--no-local-config` as a deferred peer (today redundant with `--por`).

message.content: We already are specifying 4 layers and I think local config should be able to every CLI why say local config can override "every CLI except --global-config". Also Global config can specify a different Global config, if it's circular tough shit :)

We already are specifying 4 layers and I think local config should be able to override every CLI. Why say local config can override "every CLI except --global-config". Also Global config can specify a different Global config, if it's circular tough shit :)

content.text:
Got it — let me confirm I'm reading you right before writing it up.

**Your model**: local config has *universal* precedence over CLI (and presumably env-var). No carve-outs. The project's `.vc-config.toml` is sovereign over the project's behavior; CLI is for fields the project doesn't pin. This means:

- `.vc-config.toml` says `account = "work"` → `vc-x1 init --account home foo` produces a `work` account repo. CLI loses.
- `.vc-config.toml` says `[global] config-path = "/etc/team.toml"` → `vc-x1 ... --global-config ~/mine.toml ...` loads `/etc/team.toml`. CLI loses there too.
- "Escape hatch" for ad-hoc deviations is `--no-local-config` (skip the whole local layer for this invocation).

I agree with this once I see it stated cleanly — it's the *project sovereignty* model, like `.editorconfig` or `direnv`. CLI tools usually go the other way, but those are user-facing convention-only; `vc-x1`'s `.vc-config.toml` is a deliberate per-project commitment that gets checked in, so honoring it over CLI is consistent.

**Circular global → global**: agreed, "tough shit." Loader detects a cycle (visited-set) and errors clearly; the recovery is "fix your config." No code complexity to prevent it.

**One open question: env-var placement.** With local > CLI established, where do env-vars sit? Three reasonable options:

- (a) **Local > env-var > CLI > global > error** — env-var is "session-default," still below local but above CLI. Matches "env-vars are a flavor of config, not a flavor of CLI."
- (b) **Local > CLI > env-var > global > error** — env-var is below CLI (closer to global). Useful when you want CLI to win over your shell's `VC_X1_*` setup.
- (c) **Local > env-var > global, env-var coequal with CLI** — env-var and CLI tied, one-of enforced.

The bot thinks (a) — env-var sits *with* config files (it's "ambient state"), not with CLI (which is "this invocation"). Local-config-says-X / env-var-says-Y → local wins because it's project-specific; env-var-says-X / CLI-says-Y → env-var wins because env-var was set deliberately and CLI is the per-invocation tweak.

But (a) means a typed `--account home` loses to `export VC_X1_ACCOUNT=work` in a shell — surprising to some users. (b) avoids that surprise. Your call.

Confirm (a)/(b)/(c) and I'll write up the resolution chain as a `### Resolution chain` subsection under `## Feature axes`, including:

- The 4 layers + error floor with chosen order
- `--no-local-config` / `--no-global-config` escape hatches
- `VC_X1_<KEY>` naming convention with the per-axis table
- "Circular global → global = user error" note
- Cross-link to A1 / A3 / A4 / A5 to show how each axis resolves through the chain

accounts → different remote providers), with A1 second.

### Gap list (input for close-out)

The concrete gaps for follow-up cycles to seed Todos
against:

1. **A1 has no `--dual` peer** — asymmetric defaulting.
   Add `--dual` (alias of "no `--por`") for explicit
   parity; allow `--por`/`--dual` exactly one of.
2. **A2 errors on dual** — change the error to
   "(A1=dual, A2=not-written) is impossible because dual
   needs `.vc-config.toml` for runtime topology" rather
   than the current flag-restriction message; allow
   `--config <path>` (override) under `--dual`.
3. **A3 missing `none`** — add a way to spell "no remote."
   Probably `--repo none` (orthogonal to the existing
   `remote`/`local` categories) or a separate `--no-remote`
   flag. The bot thinks `--repo none` keeps the
   one-knob-for-A3 shape.
4. **A6 is non-negotiable today** — confirm at close-out
   whether to leave it that way; if so, drop A6 from the
   axis list.
5. **User-config keys** — once A1–A5 are independent,
   wire `[default].topology` / `.write-vc-config` /
   `.remote` / `.private` into the `init` resolution
   chain. Follow the three-step `resolve_repo` shape
   (CLI → `[default]` → error).

These five become candidate `## Todo` entries at
close-out, ranked by the equalization-candidate ordering
from `## Commonality` (axis fixes that overlap with
`validate-desc` / `fix-desc` equalization land cheapest).

input.old_string: ### Gap list (input for close-out)
input.new_string:
### Resolution chain

The per-axis sections above each say "CLI → user-config →
error." That's a sketch; the full resolution chain has
four layers plus an explicit-required floor. The same chain
applies to **every axis** and every field a config carries
(account, repo category/value, private, topology, …) —
nothing axis-specific.

#### Layers (highest precedence first)

1. **CLI flag** — `--por`, `--account`, `--private`,
   `--config <path>`, `--global-config <path>`, etc. The
   per-invocation surface. CLI is god — it wins over
   every other layer when present.
2. **Environment variable** — `VC_X1_<KEY>`. The
   per-session surface, shaped by `export` or one-shot
   prefix. Loses to CLI.
3. **Local config** — `./.vc-config.toml` (the workspace
   metadata file), or whatever `--config <path>` resolved
   to. Per-project durable intent. Loses to env-var and
   CLI; **may carry any field a CLI flag exposes** — no
   carve-outs (a local config can pin
   `[global] config-path` just as it pins `account`).
4. **Global config** — `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`
   (XDG-aware), or whatever `--global-config <path>` /
   `VC_X1_GLOBAL_CONFIG` / a local-config
   `[global] config-path` resolved to. Per-user durable
   defaults. Lowest-precedence config; loses to all
   above.
5. **Error** — "no `<key>` specified; set it via CLI,
   `VC_X1_<KEY>`, `./.vc-config.toml`, or
   `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`."

Each layer is **optional**. Per-axis behavior under
the chain: the resolved value of a key is the value from
the highest layer that defines it; absence at every layer
falls to the error floor (or the axis's safe default if
one is defined — A5 template defaults to `none` because
"no template" is the only meaningful absence; no other
axis has a safe default today).

#### Escape hatches

- **`--no-local-config`** — skip layer 3 entirely for this
  invocation. Today functionally equivalent to runtime
  `--por` (because local config carries only topology
  metadata); add as a peer flag once local config gains
  non-topology fields.
- **`--no-global-config`** — skip layer 4 entirely.
  Necessary for tests / CI / "ignore my defaults this
  once."

Both flags are layer-1 (CLI) — they can't themselves be
pinned by config, since their purpose is to ignore config.

#### Redirection and cycles

Both local and global config can redirect to a different
config file via `[global] config-path = "…"` (in either
file) and `[local] config-path = "…"` (in global).
**Circular redirections are user error** — the loader
keeps a visited-set and errors with the cycle path; no
attempt is made to break the cycle automatically.

The bot thinks redirection in local pointing at a
custom global is the most useful case (a project that
ships a team-wide default file in the repo), and global
pointing at another global is mostly an unintended
consequence of letting the field exist universally.
Allowing it costs nothing.

#### Env-var naming

`VC_X1_<KEY>`, flat namespace, key matches the resolved
field name:

| Axis | Env-var | Resolves to |
| --- | --- | --- |
| A1 | `VC_X1_TOPOLOGY` | `single` \| `dual` |
| A3 account | `VC_X1_ACCOUNT` | account name |
| A3 repo | `VC_X1_REPO` | `<cat>` or `<cat>=<val>` |
| A4 | `VC_X1_PRIVATE` | `true` \| `false` |
| A5 | `VC_X1_USE_TEMPLATE` | path or `<path,path>` |
| — | `VC_X1_CONFIG` | local config path or `none` |
| — | `VC_X1_GLOBAL_CONFIG` | global config path |
| — | `VC_X1_NO_LOCAL_CONFIG` | `true` \| `false` |
| — | `VC_X1_NO_GLOBAL_CONFIG` | `true` \| `false` |

Precedent in the codebase: `config.rs:113` already honors
`XDG_CONFIG_HOME`; `test_tmp_root.rs` uses
`VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` / `VC_X1_TEST_KEEP`. The pattern is
established; `VC_X1_*` just extends it.

#### Por's view of the chain

Following the decisions in this cycle:

- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml`** (layer 3) — dual-only.
  Por never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
  `[workspace] path = "/"` write under `--por` is residue
  from when `--por` was a half-implemented opt-out and
  should be dropped.
- **User-config `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`**
  (layer 4) — topology-neutral. Both por and dual consult
  it.
- **Runtime `--por`** — overrides workspace topology
  detection across every subcommand (not just
  `init`/`clone`). In a dual workspace, runtime `--por`
  short-circuits `default_scope` → `Scope([Code])` and
  ignores `.claude/`.

### Gap list (input for close-out)

Resolution chain section structure:

- **Layers** — CLI > ENV > Local > Global > Error, each layer optional, error floor at the bottom.
- **Escape hatches** — `--no-local-config` (today redundant with runtime `--por`), `--no-global-config` (tests/CI). Both layer-1 only.
- **Redirection and cycles** — local/global can each point at a different global; visited-set detection; "circular = user error."
- **Env-var naming** — `VC_X1_<KEY>` table covering A1–A5 axes + the four config-control knobs (`VC_X1_CONFIG`, `VC_X1_GLOBAL_CONFIG`, `VC_X1_NO_LOCAL_CONFIG`, `VC_X1_NO_GLOBAL_CONFIG`).
- **Por's view of the chain** — workspace file dual-only; user-config topology-neutral; runtime `--por` overrides workspace topology across all subcommands.

**One thing I haven't done yet**: the per-axis sections (A1, A2, …) still read as "what's there today + gap" — they haven't been updated with the **decisions** we just made (add `--dual` peer, explicit-required floor, runtime `--por`).

Should I:

- (a) Add a `**Decision (0.61.0-3):**` note inline at the end of each axis section as we negotiate it. (Keeps decisions visible per-axis.)
- (b) Bundle all axis decisions into a single `### Decisions` subsection at the end, after we've walked A2–A5. (Cleaner doc structure; decisions all in one place.)
- (c) Leave the per-axis sections alone and let the close-out commit them to the new `## Todo` entries — which is the natural "downstream" of decisions anyway.

I lean (a) for A1 right now so the decisions don't get lost, then continue to A2. Your call.

content.text:
Welcome back. Picking up A2 — and I think A2 *collapses* given what we decided in A1. Let me lay out the case.

**Recap of A1 decisions** (so we're aligned):
- POR never reads or writes the workspace `.vc-config.toml`. Today's degenerate write gets dropped.
- DUAL is *detected* by the workspace `.vc-config.toml` (specifically `[workspace] other-repo`). It's the file's whole purpose.

**Consequence for A2** (`.vc-config.toml` write):
- Under A1=por: file doesn't exist. A2 is non-applicable.
- Under A1=dual: file must exist (it's how dual is detected). A2 is also non-applicable — it's always written.

So A2 isn't an independent axis at all — it's **perfectly correlated with A1**. Presence-of-`.vc-config.toml` == "this is dual."

**The `--config <path>` flag today does two things:**

1. `--config none` — skip the write. Useful only under por; under the new design por never writes it anyway, so this becomes a no-op (or rejected).
2. `--config <path>` — write content from `<path>` instead of the canned template. Useful **only if** we want non-default content in `.vc-config.toml` (e.g., a non-`.claude` companion dir name, custom workspace metadata).

The bot thinks #2 is **solving a problem we haven't stated**. Today `.claude` is hard-coded everywhere; the canned content `[workspace] path = "/", other-repo = ".claude"` is universal for dual. There's no current use case for custom-content workspace files.

**Initial decision for A2:**

- **Drop A2 as an independent axis.** It's degenerate post-A1.
- **Drop `--config <path>` entirely.** No demand for custom workspace metadata; the canned content is universal for dual. If a future need surfaces (renaming `.claude`, multi-companion workspaces), add it back as part of that feature, not as a speculative knob.
- **Keep `--config none` as a clap-rejected combination** (or just remove the flag). With por never writing the file, there's nothing for `none` to suppress.

**Open question** — am I missing a use case for custom-content workspace files? Two candidates I can think of:

- **Renaming `.claude`** — a user wanting their bot-repo to be named `.bot` or `.session` instead. Would require unhardcoding `.claude` throughout `push.rs` / `clone.rs` / etc. — a much bigger lift than just accepting a custom template.
- **Future workspace fields** — `[workspace] ochid-format` or similar, for the forks-multi-user work. Speculative; that work hasn't started.

If neither lands as a need, A2 stays collapsed. If you've got a use case I'm not seeing, surface it now.

**My recommendation: collapse A2.** Move on to A3?

So `vc-x1 init --init-from-recursive=<path>/* --repo <target>` would mean recursively copy everyting under <path> to <target>. And --init-from=<path> would be non-recursive but regex would be supported. And in it both cases multiple instances of the options would be supported. Thoughts

content.text:
I like the *direction* — generalizing template seeding to one mechanism is cleaner than inventing `--config <path>` + `--gitignore <path>` + future per-file flags. But I want to push back on scope before we fold this into A2.

**The proposal's real shape:** you're replacing all template-related knobs (`--config`, `--gitignore`, and `--use-template`) with one composable templating system. That's its own design — bigger than A2's collapse.

**What `--init-from` / `--init-from-recursive` subsumes:**

- `--config <path>` → `--init-from=<src>/.vc-config.toml`
- `--gitignore <path>` → `--init-from=<src>/.gitignore`
- `--use-template code-tpl/` → `--init-from-recursive=code-tpl/*`
- Any future "seed file X" need → just point `--init-from` at it

That's a real win. One mechanism, no per-file flag explosion.

**Open questions I'd surface before designing:**

1. **Glob vs regex syntax.** You wrote "non-recursive but regex would be supported" — what's the exact syntax? Shell-glob (`*.toml`, expanded by the shell, vc-x1 sees the expanded list)? Or regex inside the path string (`<dir>/.*\.toml`, parsed by vc-x1)? I lean shell-glob — let the shell do globbing, vc-x1 just gets file paths. Regex-in-path is unusual and inconsistent with `cp` / `rsync` conventions users expect.

2. **Per-side targeting under dual.** `vc-x1 init foo` produces *two* destinations (code repo + `.claude/`). Where does `--init-from=<src>` land?
   - (a) Code by default; `--init-from-bot=<src>` for the companion.
   - (b) `--init-from=<src>::<dest>` syntax where `<dest>` is `code` / `bot`.
   - (c) Source layout mirrors target (`<src>/code/...` → code repo, `<src>/.claude/...` → companion).
   I lean (c) — the source dir's own layout encodes the target. No flag explosion, naturally extensible.

3. **Conflict resolution.** Multiple `--init-from` invocations land the same file (`a/.gitignore` and `b/.gitignore`). Last wins? First wins? Error?

4. **Canned writes vs templates.** Today init writes canned `.vc-config.toml` + `.gitignore`. With templating:
   - (a) Templates land first, canned fills only what templates didn't provide. (Templates "override" canned.)
   - (b) Any `--init-from` suppresses canned entirely.
   - (c) Canned never runs if templates exist.
   I lean (a) — predictable, additive, no special-casing.

5. **Mandatory dual fields.** Dual *requires* `.vc-config.toml` with `[workspace] other-repo`. If user's template lacks it, init should detect and fail with a clear error (not write a broken dual).

**Scope recommendation:**

Don't fold the templating design into A2. The audit doc should stay focused on **topology parity** — the question that motivated the cycle. Templating is its own feature.

Concrete proposal:

- **A2 collapse stays as-is**: write of `.vc-config.toml` is perfectly correlated with A1. Drop A2 as an independent axis.
- **Drop the speculative `--config <path>` capability** from A2's section in the doc.
- **Add a stub** to the audit doc (a brief paragraph under A2 or in the Gap list) noting: "the *capability* `--config <path>` was meant to serve (custom workspace metadata) is deferred to a broader init-templating design — see `notes/init-templating.md` (TBD)."
- **Spin `notes/init-templating.md`** as a separate design note capturing the `--init-from` / `--init-from-recursive` proposal, the questions above, and the eventual rollout. This becomes its own cycle in 0.62.0+ — independent of the parity equalization work.

Either I create the stub `notes/init-templating.md` now (within `-4` or a new commit) with the proposal + open questions, or we defer the note entirely to that future cycle. **My preference: create the stub now while context is fresh** — drops it into the design space so future-you finds it. ~30 lines.

Thoughts? Move on to A3 either way?

content.text:
Got it. Let me confirm interpretations + raise one nuance before writing the stub:

1. **Glob** ✓ (shell-glob, vc-x1 receives expanded paths).
2. **Scope** — I'll go with the variant-in-command-name approach: `--init-from-code=<glob>` and `--init-from-bot=<glob>`, with bare `--init-from=<glob>` defaulting to code (since por only has code). Rules out the `--scope` clash with the existing runtime `--scope` flag. Clean and clap-friendly.
3. **Last wins + warning on collision** ✓.
4. **Any `--init-from*` suppresses canned writes entirely** ✓ — clean. User opts into full control once they engage templating.
5. **One pushback**: you said *warning* if `.vc-config.toml` lookup fails after copy. For **dual specifically** I think it should be a **hard error**, not a warning:

   - Dual without `.vc-config.toml` is a broken workspace — `default_scope` / `scope_to_repos` won't detect the topology, every downstream subcommand will treat it as por.
   - Warn-and-create gets you a workspace that *looks* dual (has `.claude/`) but *acts* por at runtime. Surprising and hard to debug.
   - Refusing to create a broken workspace is the right call.

   For **por**: no `.vc-config.toml` is expected; no warning needed.

   Concretely: after `--init-from*` runs, if A1=dual and the resolved workspace config file (default name `.vc-config.toml`, overridable via [some future mechanism]) doesn't exist or doesn't have `[workspace] other-repo`, error out before continuing. **Warn** vs **error**: your call.

**Default-name overridability** (your #5 hedge): I think the workspace-config filename should stay fixed at `.vc-config.toml` for now — overriding it ripples through every subcommand's lookup code. If someone wants a non-default name later, that's a real feature with real cost; don't bake the flexibility in speculatively. So the dual check becomes "after copy, does `.vc-config.toml` exist in the code dir with `[workspace] other-repo` set" — fixed name, no override mechanism.

**Plan:**

1. Create `notes/init-templating.md` — design stub capturing what we've negotiated. ~50 lines.
2. Update A2's section in the audit doc — note A2 collapses and link to the templating stub.
3. Move to A3.

That's a `-4` Work commit. Sound good? (If yes, I'll also need a quick call on the dual warn-vs-error.)

Accepted — that's the better shape. "Init is for setup; downstream commands enforce correctness" is consistent with how the rest of the chain works (e.g., `default_scope` errors when `--scope=bot` requested but no `other-repo`).

Implementation note worth capturing: `default_scope` today *silently* returns `Scope([Code])` when `.vc-config.toml` is missing. That's exactly the "looks dual, acts por" failure mode. To make your warn-then-error work, `default_scope` (or a new check) needs to detect *broken-dual* state — `.claude/` exists in the workspace but `.vc-config.toml` doesn't — and refuse. Today's silent fallback masks the very thing we'd want to catch.

input.content:
# init templating (design stub)

Forward-looking design capture from the 0.61.0 parity audit
cycle. No implementation yet — concrete work lands when a
follow-on cycle picks it up. Generalizes seed-file handling
in `vc-x1 init` (and by extension `vc-x1 clone`) into one
composable templating mechanism that subsumes today's
`--config <path>` (deprecated under A2 collapse — see
[[1]]) and a hypothetical `--gitignore <path>`.

## Goal

One mechanism for "seed files into the workspace at init
time" rather than per-file flags. The user supplies one or
more source paths; init copies them into the new workspace
during the seed phase, before commits. Subsumes today's
`--use-template <CODE[,BOT]>` as the natural shorthand.

## Surface

- `--init-from-code=<glob>` — copy files matched by
  `<glob>` into the code repo (non-recursive).
- `--init-from-bot=<glob>` — same, into the `.claude/`
  companion. Only meaningful under dual.
- `--init-from=<glob>` — shorthand for `--init-from-code`
  (since por only has code, and dual users typically seed
  code more often than bot).
- `--init-from-recursive=<glob>` /
  `--init-from-code-recursive=<glob>` /
  `--init-from-bot-recursive=<glob>` — recursive variants.

Each flag accepts a **shell glob** (expansion done by the
shell; `vc-x1` receives the expanded path list). Each may
be specified multiple times — the result is the additive
union of all sources. Order matters for collisions (see
below).

## Behavior

- **Copy semantics** — files only; preserves relative
  layout under the source root.
- **Collision: last writer wins, with a warning.** If two
  `--init-from*` flags resolve to the same destination
  file, the later one overwrites the earlier; `vc-x1`
  emits a warning naming both sources. The bot thinks
  warnings are right here — silent overwrite is a
  footgun; erroring would block valid layering use cases
  (a base template + per-project tweaks).
- **Canned writes suppressed when templating is engaged.**
  Today `init` writes a canned `.vc-config.toml` and
  `.gitignore`. With any `--init-from*` present, **both
  canned writes are suppressed entirely**. The user opted
  in to full control of the seeded content; init doesn't
  layer canned defaults underneath their template.
- **Dual post-templating check (deferred).** After
  templating completes for a dual workspace, if
  `.vc-config.toml` is missing or lacks
  `[workspace] other-repo`, init **warns** (does not
  error). The workspace looks dual (has `.claude/`) but
  acts por at runtime; downstream subcommands enforce
  the invariant. See [Deferred-validation](#deferred-validation).

## Deferred-validation

Init's `.vc-config.toml` check is a **warning**, not an
error, to support workflows where the user plans to
supply the config post-init (e.g. checking in the
workspace shell first, then populating config files in
a follow-up commit).

The error happens at the **first downstream invocation**:
`default_scope` (or a new explicit check) detects the
broken-dual state — `.claude/` exists but `.vc-config.toml`
doesn't (or lacks `other-repo`) — and refuses to proceed
with a clear error pointing at the missing file.

Today `default_scope` silently falls back to
`Scope([Code])` when `.vc-config.toml` is absent
(`src/common.rs:594`). That silent fallback is exactly
the "looks dual, acts por" surprise this design avoids;
fixing it is a prerequisite for the post-init templating
flow.

## Fixed filename

`.vc-config.toml` stays the fixed name; overriding it is
deferred until a real use case surfaces (would ripple
through every subcommand's lookup code — `default_scope`,
`find_workspace_root_from`, `validate-desc`, `fix-desc`,
`push`, `clone`, …).

## Subsumes

- `--config <path>` — never built; A2's collapse left
  the capability un-claimed. Equivalent under templating:
  `--init-from=<src>/.vc-config.toml`.
- `--gitignore <path>` — never built; equivalent under
  templating: `--init-from=<src>/.gitignore`.
- `--use-template <CODE[,BOT]>` — today's coarse
  whole-repo template flag. Subsumed by recursive
  variants:
  - `--use-template code-tpl/` →
    `--init-from-code-recursive=code-tpl/*`.
  - `--use-template code-tpl/,bot-tpl/` → two flags:
    `--init-from-code-recursive=code-tpl/*` +
    `--init-from-bot-recursive=bot-tpl/*`.

The bot thinks `--use-template` can be retired once
templating ships, but a back-compat shim that translates
old form → new form is cheap and worth keeping for one
release cycle.

## Open questions (resolve at design time)

- **Variable substitution.** Should the copy step support
  variable substitution (`{{name}}`, `{{account}}`)? The
  bot thinks no — keep the mechanism a pure copy. Users
  who need substitution can pre-process and pass the
  result. Adding substitution invites a templating-language
  rabbit hole.
- **Symbolic links and executable bits.** Preserve, or
  flatten? Probably preserve — `cp -a` semantics. Worth
  confirming.
- **Source-not-found behavior.** Hard error (typo
  protection) or soft skip (let globs return empty)?
  Probably hard error — a missing source is almost
  always user mistake. Empty glob expansion (the shell
  passing zero paths) is fine.
- **Interaction with `vc-x1 clone`.** Clone seeds from an
  existing remote, so templating overlays *on top of* the
  cloned content. Same flag set, same collision rules.

## See also

- `notes/por-dual-parity-audit.md > ## Feature axes > A2`
  — the collapse that motivates this design [[1]].
- `notes/por-dual-parity-audit.md > ## Feature axes > A5`
  — `--use-template` today; this design subsumes it.

# References

[1]: /notes/por-dual-parity-audit.md#a2-vc-configtoml-write
